_scoring_convention.political_risk_score: 0–100 where 100 = zero political risk (higher is better)
_scoring_convention.operational_drag_score: 0–100 where 100 = maximum drag / worst efficiency (higher is worse)
_scoring_convention.rag_convention: GREEN = proceed, AMBER = proceed with conditions, RED = material concern

jurisdiction.name: Bulgaria
jurisdiction.currency_eur_rate_note: 1 EUR = 1.95583 BGN — FIXED rate under Bulgaria's Currency Board Arrangement (CBA), in force since 1 July 1999. This is a legally fixed rate, not a market rate. The CBA pegs the BGN to the EUR at an irrevocable rate set by the Bulgarian National Bank (BNB). FX risk is negligible for operational cashflows. Eurozone accession: Bulgaria has been pursuing Euro adoption — verify current accession status with BNB / ECB as of analysis date.
jurisdiction.currency_eur_rate_date: Fixed — Currency Board rate unchanged since 1 July 1999
jurisdiction.currency_eur_rate_source: Bulgarian National Bank (BNB) — bnb.bg; Law on the Bulgarian National Bank (Article 29 — currency board provisions)
jurisdiction.data_staleness_warning: Bulgarian RES auction clearing prices are published by EWRC (Energy and Water Regulatory Commission). CIT rate and support scheme parameters should be verified at EWRC (dker.bg) and NRA (nap.bg) before use. Euro adoption status must be verified at BNB / ECB as of analysis date.

primary_energy_legislation.instrument: Law on Energy from Renewable Sources (Закон за енергията от възобновяеми източници — ZEVI), promulgated 2011, as amended
primary_energy_legislation.relevance: Primary RES support law; establishes competitive auction framework; defines feed-in premium mechanism; transposes EU Renewable Energy Directives

primary_energy_legislation.instrument: Energy Act (Закон за енергетиката — ZE) 2003, as amended
primary_energy_legislation.relevance: Framework energy law; licensing; grid connection rights; EWRC regulatory authority powers

primary_energy_legislation.instrument: National Recovery and Resilience Plan (NRRP) — Bulgaria 2021–2026
primary_energy_legislation.relevance: EU-funded investment framework; includes RES capacity targets and permitting reform commitments

permitting_legislation.instrument: Spatial Planning Act (Закон за устройство на територията — ZUT)
permitting_legislation.relevance: Zoning and spatial planning; construction permits; primary permitting framework for RES projects

permitting_legislation.instrument: Environmental Protection Act (Закон за опазване на околната среда — ZOOOS) and EIA Ordinance
permitting_legislation.relevance: EIA requirements; Natura 2000 assessment; mandatory for RES projects above threshold

tax_legislation.instrument: Corporate Income Tax Act (Закон за корпоративното подоходно облагане — ZKPO) — 10% flat rate
tax_legislation.relevance: 10% flat CIT rate — one of the lowest in the EU; standard depreciation provisions

tax_legislation.instrument: Value Added Tax Act (Закон за данък върху добавената стойност — ZDDS)

subsidy_control_legislation.relevance: EU framework for assessing Bulgarian RES support compatibility
Block exemptions for smaller RES measures

fdi_legislation.instrument: Investment Promotion Act (Закон за насърчаване на инвестициите — ZNI)
fdi_legislation.relevance: Investment promotion; Class A/B/C certification for prioritised investments; InvestBulgaria Agency

fdi_legislation.instrument: EU FDI Screening Regulation (EU) 2019/452 — implemented via Ministry of Economy
fdi_legislation.relevance: Bulgaria has implemented basic FDI screening under EU Regulation 2019/452; energy critical infrastructure included

permitting._notes: Bulgarian permitting is among the more complex in the EU for RES. Multi-agency process involves EWRC (licence), Ministry of Energy, regional/municipal authorities, and environmental authorities. Corruption risk in local administration is a material consideration. P50 timelines run from formal application.

solar_utility.primary_authority: EWRC (licence); Regional Inspectorate for Environment and Water (RIEW) for EIA; Municipal council for spatial plan amendment; DNSC (construction supervision)
solar_utility.primary_process: EWRC Production Licence → Spatial Plan Amendment (ZUT) → EIA (ZOOOS) → Building Permit → Grid Connection Agreement → Commissioning Act
solar_utility.appeal_mechanism: Administrative Courts (Административни съдилища); Supreme Administrative Court (Върховен административен съд — VAS)
solar_utility.notes: Bulgaria has strong solar resource (1,400–1,600 kWh/kWp p.a. in southern regions). Grid connection capacity is constrained particularly in southwestern and southeastern regions.

onshore_wind.primary_authority: EWRC; RIEW for EIA; Municipal authorities; Ministry of Defence for aviation/radar clearances
onshore_wind.primary_process: EWRC Production Licence → EIA → Spatial Plan Amendment → Building Permit → Grid Connection → Commissioning
onshore_wind.setback_rules: 500m from residential areas typical; Natura 2000 constraints material (significant bird migration routes through Bulgaria)
onshore_wind.notes: Bulgaria has good wind resource in northern coastal areas (Dobrudzha) and in the Rhodope Mountains. Natura 2000 is a significant permitting constraint — Bulgaria has one of the largest Natura 2000 networks in the EU by territory percentage. Bird migration routes (Via Pontica, Via Aristotelis) create species protection constraints for wind sites.

offshore_wind.applicable_note: Bulgaria has Black Sea coastline. Offshore wind framework is nascent — no projects commissioned. Black Sea offshore development faces specific regulatory gaps. Not a viable near-term market.
offshore_wind.notes: Black Sea offshore wind is a long-term aspiration. Regulatory framework under development. Not material for near-term capital deployment.

battery_storage_utility.primary_process: EWRC licence; EIA if above threshold; building permit; grid connection
battery_storage_utility.notes: BESS market in Bulgaria is nascent. Grid stability in northeastern Bulgaria (wind-heavy Dobrudzha region) creates demand for storage.

transmission_operators.name: ESO EAD (Електроенергиен системен оператор — Electricity System Operator)

grid_connection.distribution_operators: Electrorazpredelenie Yug AD (EVN — southern Bulgaria); Electrorazpredelenie Sever AD (EON / Chez — northern Bulgaria); Electrorazpredelenie Sofia Oblast AD (Energo-Pro); CEZ Razpredelenie Bulgaria AD (western Bulgaria, Prague-owned being divested)
grid_connection.regulatory_authority: EWRC (Комисия за енергийно и водно регулиране — Energy and Water Regulatory Commission)
grid_connection.legal_basis: Energy Act ZE §§82–92 (grid connection rights); ZEVI §§42–50 (priority access for RES); EWRC Network Codes

priority_access.mechanism: Priority dispatch for RES under ZEVI and EU Electricity Directive 2019/944 (transposed)
priority_access.description: RES generators have statutory priority dispatch. Curtailment compensation framework exists.

grid_congestion.notes: Dobrudzha (northeastern Bulgaria) has best wind resource but faces grid capacity constraints from existing wind installations. Solar development in southern Bulgaria faces DSO capacity limits in some areas. ESO reinforcement programme underway but slow.

connection_cost_bgn_per_mw.fx_note: BGN/EUR at fixed Currency Board rate of 1 EUR = 1.95583 BGN
connection_cost_bgn_per_mw.notes: Connection costs regulated by EWRC. Reinforcement costs socialised via regulated tariffs.

grid_connection.operational_drag_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH — grid constraints in wind/solar resource regions; ESO reinforcement behind demand

support_mechanisms._rule_3a_note: Rule 3A: The Bulgarian RES auction premium under ZEVI (as amended to comply with CEEAG) is a ONE-WAY market premium. Premium = max(0, reference price − market price). When market price exceeds the reference price, premium is zero — generators retain full merchant upside with NO clawback. NOTE: Verify current mechanism structure with Bulgarian legal counsel — Bulgaria has undergone multiple legislative reforms and the precise mechanism structure should be confirmed against the current ZEVI text and EWRC decisions.
support_mechanisms.primary_mechanism: Feed-in Premium (Премия за производство на електрическа енергия) — competitive auction
support_mechanisms.administrator: EWRC (sets parameters); ESO (administers premium payments to generators)
support_mechanisms.legal_basis: ZEVI §§32–38 (as amended); EWRC Decisions on auction parameters

mechanism_description.summary: Generator wins competitive auction and receives a reference price. Sells power on IBEX (Bulgarian Energy Exchange) day-ahead market. Premium = max(0, reference price − market price). No clawback when market exceeds reference price.
mechanism_description.upside_treatment: UNCAPPED — premium is zero when market price exceeds reference price; generator retains full market revenue
mechanism_description.downside_treatment: PROTECTED — ESO pays the shortfall when market price falls below reference price
mechanism_description.verification_note: Bulgarian RES support legislation has been amended multiple times. Confirm current mechanism type (one-way vs. two-way), contract term, and reference price with EWRC and local legal counsel before project commitment.

auction.frequency: Limited — Bulgarian auction programme has been slow to develop; verify current auction calendar at dker.bg

reference_price_bgn_mwh_approx._staleness_warning: Bulgarian reference prices are set per auction round. Very limited public auction history. Figures are indicative only — verify at EWRC official publications (dker.bg).

year_2014.description: Bulgaria imposed a 20% levy (вноска) on revenues from FiT contracts for RES generators commissioned before 2014 under Law on Energy from Renewable Sources. The levy applied retroactively to already-contracted FiT revenues.
year_2014.legal_basis: Amendments to ZEVI and subsidiary legislation enacted 2014
year_2014.impact: Material financial losses; multiple ICSID and ad hoc arbitration proceedings against Bulgaria; severe chilling effect on FDI in Bulgarian renewables
year_2014.severity: HIGH — retroactive modification of contracted FiT revenues; one of the most significant retroactive RES interventions in the EU alongside Czech solar levy and Greek FiT haircuts
year_2014.current_status: Levy was later modified and some relief granted following arbitration outcomes. Post-2014 framework is more stable but legacy risk discount applies.

tax._rule_3b_note: Rule 3B: Bulgarian depreciation timing benefit must be calculated on 10% CIT base — low rate means smaller absolute timing benefit vs. higher-tax EU peers.

corporation_tax_structure.legal_basis: Corporate Income Tax Act (ZKPO) — 10% flat CIT rate; one of the lowest in the EU alongside Ireland (12.5%) and Hungary (9%)
corporation_tax_structure.eu_parent_subsidiary_exemption: 0% WHT for qualifying EU parent companies under EU Parent-Subsidiary Directive

combined_effective_tax_rate_range_pct.notes: Bulgaria has no regional trade tax. Effective rate close to 10% statutory CIT. Very competitive by EU standards.

standard_depreciation.method: straight-line as standard; accelerated available for certain qualifying assets

npv_benefit_analysis.description: At 10% CIT and 10% WACC: straight-line over 10 years (10% p.a.) NPV of tax shield ≈ 6.1% of qualifying CapEx. Low CIT rate limits the absolute timing benefit — primary CapEx incentive is the low 10% rate itself, not depreciation acceleration.
npv_benefit_analysis.note: For Bulgaria, the 10% flat CIT rate is the dominant tax incentive — not specific depreciation acceleration.

investment_incentive_class_a.description: Class A certified investments under Investment Promotion Act (ZNI) may receive accelerated administrative procedures, customised infrastructure support, and potential employment subsidies
investment_incentive_class_a.legal_basis: Investment Promotion Act (ZNI); InvestBulgaria Agency regulations
investment_incentive_class_a.threshold_for_certification: Large qualifying investments — thresholds vary by project type and region

ppa_market.legal_basis: Obligations and Contracts Act (Закон за задълженията и договорите — ZZD); contractual freedom; no bespoke PPA statute
ppa_market.jurisdiction_enforcement: Bulgarian courts are functional but slower than western European peers. Commercial disputes: 2–5 years. Sofia Arbitration Court (SAC) available. ICC/Vienna arbitration recommended for international transactions.
ppa_market.market_liquidity: Limited — corporate PPA market in early development
ppa_market.prominent_corporate_offtakers: Limited pool — Bulgarian industrial companies, regional manufacturers, international corporations with Bulgarian operations
ppa_market.credit_risk_note: Bulgarian corporate credit market is thin. International corporate offtakers or parent guarantee strongly recommended for debt financing.
ppa_market.cross_border_enforceability: Medium — EU enforcement regime applies; NY Convention signatory; practical enforcement speed is a concern

credit_ratings.source_note: Bulgaria maintains solid investment-grade ratings. Verify current ratings at agency websites.
credit_ratings._staleness_warning: Ratings subject to ongoing review — verify before use.

sovereign_risk.political_stability: Medium — Bulgaria has experienced significant political instability with multiple elections and short-lived governments since 2021; anti-corruption reform demands have created governing coalition fragility
sovereign_risk.government_structure: Parliamentary republic; unicameral National Assembly (Народно събрание); Constitutional Court
sovereign_risk.regulatory_track_record: Poor for RES sector — 2014 retroactive levy is defining legacy risk event; improving under EU NRRP commitments

retroactive_policy_incidents.description: 20% levy imposed on FiT revenues for commissioned RES projects — retroactive modification of contracted revenues
retroactive_policy_incidents.severity: HIGH — among the most significant retroactive RES interventions in EU history

sovereign_risk.expropriation_risk: Low — EU member state; property rights under ECHR and Bulgarian Constitution
sovereign_risk.currency_risk: Negligible — BGN is fixed to EUR via Currency Board at 1.95583 BGN/EUR since 1999. For all practical purposes equivalent to EUR-denominated until formal Eurozone accession.
sovereign_risk.political_notes: Bulgaria's political instability is the primary near-term sovereign concern. Multiple elections since 2021 have made sustained policy reform difficult. EU accession to Eurozone and Schengen (border controls element achieved December 2024) represent positive structural progress. Anti-corruption reform progress is a condition for continued EU funds release.
sovereign_risk.score_rationale: Deductions from 100: 2014 retroactive RES levy (-20), political instability / frequent elections (-10), corruption risk in local administration (-6), slower judiciary (-4). Score 60 reflects improving trajectory but significant legacy discount.

grid_connection_drag.assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH — northeastern grid constrained by existing wind; solar grid limits in some regions; ESO reinforcement programme slow

permitting_drag_solar.assessment: MEDIUM — multi-agency process; corruption risk in local planning adds uncertainty; manageable with experienced Bulgarian development team

permitting_drag_onshore_wind.assessment: HIGH — Natura 2000 (large network); Via Pontica bird migration; multi-agency process; 4–6 year realistic P50

bureaucratic_friction.assessment: HIGH — Bulgarian public administration capacity limited; corruption risk in local permitting is a material consideration (Bulgaria remains under EU EPPO monitoring)

judicial_appeal_risk.assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH — administrative courts functional; challenge risk from environmental NGOs increasing; Natura 2000 challenges are primary judicial risk

operational_drag.primary_bottleneck: Multi-agency permitting complexity combined with corruption risk in local administration; Natura 2000 bird migration constraints for wind
operational_drag.score_rationale: Score 72 reflects a high-drag jurisdiction where experienced local development partnerships are essential to navigate administrative complexity.

fdi_restrictions.screening_regime: EU FDI Screening Regulation (EU) 2019/452 — Bulgaria has implemented basic notification framework
fdi_restrictions.practical_risk: Low-Medium for OECD investors; elevated for non-EU/non-OECD
fdi_restrictions.notes: Bulgaria has implemented FDI screening but the framework is less developed than western EU peers. Energy critical infrastructure notification applies. Low prohibition risk for OECD investors.

zevi_state_aid_status.status: Bulgarian RES auction scheme under CEEAG framework — competitive auction mechanism satisfies proportionality. Verify current EC clearance status at EC State Aid register (competition.ec.europa.eu).
zevi_state_aid_status.retroactive_levy_note: The 2014 retroactive levy was a domestic fiscal measure — NOT an EC State Aid recovery order. Domestic retroactivity risk and EC State Aid recovery risk are distinct vectors.

retrospective_clawback_risk.assessment: Low for post-2014 auction-based projects under cleared framework

financial_model_inputs._rule_3d_note: Rule 3D: Inputs for IRR, NPV, CapEx payback, DSCR, and LCOE modelling.
financial_model_inputs._rule_3c_note: BGN is the domestic currency. All BGN figures converted to EUR at FIXED Currency Board rate of 1 EUR = 1.95583 BGN. No FX rate risk for EUR investors given currency board — but Eurozone accession status must be verified.

illustrative_wind_fip.note: Dobrudzha northeastern sites have best wind resource (30–38% capacity factors).

permitting_window_solar.rationale: Manageable but multi-agency complexity and corruption risk require experienced local team; 24–48 month P50

permitting_window_onshore_wind.rationale: Natura 2000 (Via Pontica migration) and multi-agency process create real constraints; 4–6 year P50; feasible but complex

grid_connection.rationale: Grid capacity constrained in primary resource regions; ESO reinforcement behind demand; manageable with advance planning

support_mechanism_availability.rationale: ZEVI auction framework improving; limited auction history; reference price verification essential; mechanism type confirmation with local counsel required

tax_efficiency.rationale: 10% CIT is among the lowest in EU; negligible FX risk given BGN/EUR currency board; very competitive tax environment

ppa_enforceability.rationale: ZZD provides contractual framework; courts slower than western Europe; thin corporate offtaker market; ICC arbitration recommended

political_risk.rationale: 2014 retroactive levy precedent is material; political instability ongoing; corruption risk; improving under EU accountability frameworks

retroactive_policy_risk.rationale: 2014 retroactive RES levy is a mandatory investor disclosure item; while post-2014 framework is more stable, the precedent is severe

operational_drag.rationale: Score 72 — high bureaucratic friction and Natura 2000 constraints are real; solar more viable than wind

fdi_restrictions.rationale: Basic FDI screening applies; low prohibition risk for OECD investors; InvestBulgaria Agency provides support

eu_state_aid_risk.rationale: ZEVI auction framework EC-cleared; low formal State Aid recovery risk

rag_matrix.overall_rationale: Bulgaria offers the lowest CIT rate of the 11 notable absences (10%), an effectively EUR-pegged currency, and good solar resource (1,400–1,600 kWh/kWp). The combination of the 2014 retroactive RES levy precedent (mandatory disclosure), political instability, corruption risk in local administration, and multi-agency permitting complexity means this is a jurisdiction requiring experienced local development partnerships and higher return expectations. Best suited to Value-Add / Development risk profiles.

comparison_table.2_grid_connection_cost_eur: EUR 30,679–204,581 per MW (at fixed 1 EUR = 1.95583 BGN)
comparison_table.2_grid_connection_cost_fx_date: Fixed Currency Board rate — no FX risk
comparison_table.4_effective_tax_rate_note: 10% flat CIT (ZKPO). No regional trade tax. Lowest CIT among the 11 notable absences.
comparison_table.4_key_incentive: 10% flat CIT is the primary incentive. Class A investment certification (ZNI) for large qualifying projects.
comparison_table.5_ppa_legal_basis: Obligations and Contracts Act (ZZD); ICC/SAC arbitration recommended
comparison_table.8_revenue_floor_native: BGN 120/MWh approx. (solar, indicative reference price — very limited auction history)
comparison_table.8_revenue_floor_staleness_warning: Bulgarian auction programme has very limited history. Verify at EWRC (dker.bg) before use.

disclaimer: This output is produced by Hyperion AI for indicative jurisdiction-screening purposes only. It does not constitute legal or financial advice. Users should obtain qualified local legal and financial counsel before committing capital. Hyperion AI complies with SRA Standards and Regulations (15/12/24 edition).

green_hydrogen.primary_support_mechanism: Bulgaria participates in IPCEI Hy2Tech (2022). National hydrogen strategy in development. EU cohesion funds available for hydrogen projects.
green_hydrogen.notes: Bulgaria is an IPCEI Hy2Tech participant. Hydrogen regulatory framework at early stage. High solar irradiation supports electrolysis economics. Verify current national subsidy programme before use.

floating_solar.primary_support_mechanism: Market premium under Zakon za energiata ot vaznobnovyaemi iztochnitsi (ZEVI) — Renewable Energy Sources Act.
floating_solar.notes: Permitting estimated as ground-mounted solar P50 plus 9 months for water authority consent. Irradiance comparable to ground-mounted at same latitude per JRC PVGIS. Verify national tender eligibility before use.

active_smr_programmes: Kozloduy NPP Units 5 & 6 (VVER-1000) operational; new VVER-1200 Unit 7 proposed
nuclear_smr.notes: Bulgaria operates Kozloduy NPP and is considering expansion with new VVER-1200 unit. No specific SMR programme, but nuclear expansion policy is active. Source: IEA country review.

lng_gas.notes: No LNG terminal. Black Sea coastline could support terminal development. Receives gas via TurkStream and Interconnector Greece-Bulgaria (IGB).

data_centre.notes: Insufficient published data for medium-confidence estimate.

natural_gas_lng.notes: Bulgaria has gas-fired generation. EU Taxonomy Delegated Act (EU 2022/1214) transition eligibility applies for qualifying CCGT below 270gCO2e/kWh. No formal capacity market confirmed. Heavily coal-dependent grid undergoing transition. CCGT CapEx estimate: EUR 900,000/MW (IEA mid-point reference).

biowaste_energy.notes: Insufficient published data for medium-confidence assessment. EU RED III compliance and feedstock sustainability criteria apply. Verify current Bulgarian bioenergy support framework before use.

coal_energy.notes: Bulgaria has significant coal capacity (Maritsa East complex). No formal phase-out commitment. EU Taxonomy-excluded. Wholesale merchant revenue only.
